name: Containers & Kubernetes
description: Containers and orchestration: images, pods, controllers, networking and operations. 30-question seed set, growing to 300.

questions.text: A container differs from a virtual machine because it…
options: Shares the host OS kernel instead of running its own
Includes a full guest operating system
Always runs faster regardless of workload
Cannot be moved between machines in practice as a rule
questions.explanation: Containers package the app and deps but share the host kernel — lighter and faster to start than a VM.

questions.text: A Docker image is…
options: A read-only template used to create containers
A running instance of a container
A network bridge between containers
A log of container activity
questions.explanation: The image is the blueprint; a container is a running instance of it.

questions.text: A Dockerfile is used to…
options: Route traffic between services in practice
Store secrets for the container
Define how to build an image step by step
Monitor a running container's health
questions.explanation: Each instruction becomes a layer; the file is the reproducible recipe for the image.

questions.text: In Kubernetes, the smallest deployable unit is…
options: A service
A pod
A container
A node
questions.explanation: A pod wraps one or more containers that share network and storage — Kubernetes schedules pods, not bare containers.

questions.text: A Kubernetes Deployment is responsible for…
options: Building container images in practice as a rule in practice
Storing persistent data for pods
Keeping a desired number of pod replicas running
Routing external traffic to services
questions.explanation: Declare how many replicas you want; the Deployment reconciles reality toward it, including rollouts.

questions.text: A Kubernetes Service exists to…
options: Store configuration as key-value pairs
Build and push images automatically
Give pods a stable network endpoint
Limit how much CPU a pod can use
questions.explanation: Pods come and go with changing IPs; a Service is the stable name and address in front of them.

questions.text: kubectl is…
options: A container runtime like Docker
The cluster's internal database
The Kubernetes scheduler component
The command-line tool to talk to a cluster
questions.explanation: Your client for the API server: get, apply, describe, logs — the day-to-day interface.

questions.text: A container registry stores…
options: Pod logs and metrics
Cluster configuration and secrets
Built images ready to be pulled
Running containers awaiting scheduling
questions.explanation: Docker Hub, ECR, ACR, GHCR — you push images there and clusters pull them to run.

questions.text: A Kubernetes namespace is used to…
options: Encrypt traffic between pods
Divide a cluster into isolated logical groups
Store persistent volumes
Define container build steps in practice as a rule
questions.explanation: Logical partitioning for teams or environments, with their own quotas and access.

questions.text: Docker Compose is typically used to…
options: Orchestrate thousands of nodes in production
Define and run multi-container apps locally
Replace Kubernetes in production clusters
Scan images for vulnerabilities
questions.explanation: One YAML file to spin up an app plus its database and cache on a dev machine.

questions.text: A ReplicaSet's job is to…
options: Store configuration for pods
Ensure a specified number of pod copies exist
Expose pods to external traffic in practice as a rule
Roll out new versions gradually
questions.explanation: Deployments manage ReplicaSets, which in turn keep the exact replica count alive.

questions.text: A ConfigMap holds…
options: Persistent application data in practice
Container image layers
Sensitive secrets like passwords
Non-confidential configuration data
questions.explanation: Config decoupled from images; for secrets you use a Secret object instead.

questions.text: A liveness probe tells Kubernetes…
options: Whether a container should be restarted
When to scale the deployment up
How much memory a pod is using
Whether a pod is ready to receive traffic
questions.explanation: Liveness failing → restart the container. Readiness is the separate 'ready for traffic' check.

questions.text: A readiness probe failing causes Kubernetes to…
options: Delete and recreate the pod
Scale the deployment down
Remove the pod from Service endpoints
Restart the failing container immediately
questions.explanation: Not ready → traffic stops flowing to it, but it isn't killed — it may just need warm-up time.

questions.text: A StatefulSet is preferred over a Deployment when pods need…
options: Fewer resources than usual
To run as briefly as possible
To be completely stateless
Stable identities and persistent per-pod storage
questions.explanation: Databases and clustered apps need stable names and their own volumes across restarts.

questions.text: A DaemonSet ensures that…
options: Pods restart on a fixed schedule
A copy of a pod runs on every (or selected) node
Traffic is balanced across pods in practice as a rule
Exactly one replica runs cluster-wide
questions.explanation: Perfect for per-node agents: log shippers, monitoring, CNI components.

questions.text: A multi-stage Docker build primarily reduces…
options: Build time on the CI server
The final image size by discarding build tooling
The number of running containers
Network traffic during pulls in practice as a rule
questions.explanation: Compile in a fat stage, copy only the artefact into a slim final stage — smaller, safer images.

questions.text: An Ingress in Kubernetes provides…
options: HTTP routing to services by host and path
Automatic image builds in practice as a rule
Pod-to-pod network encryption
Persistent storage for stateful apps
questions.explanation: One entry point with rules routing to backing Services — TLS termination usually lives here too.

questions.text: A Kubernetes Secret, by default, stores data…
options: Encrypted with a per-cluster master key
Base64-encoded, not truly encrypted at rest
Only in the pod's memory in practice as a rule
In plaintext visible to all namespaces
questions.explanation: Base64 is encoding, not encryption; enable encryption-at-rest and RBAC to actually protect them.

questions.text: A resource 'request' versus 'limit' in Kubernetes means…
options: Minimum guaranteed versus maximum allowed
CPU versus memory allocation
Preferred versus required nodes
Startup versus running allocation in practice
questions.explanation: The scheduler places by requests; the kubelet throttles or kills at limits.

questions.text: A pod stuck in CrashLoopBackOff means…
options: It is waiting for a persistent volume
Its image cannot be pulled from the registry
It cannot be scheduled onto any node
It keeps starting, failing and being restarted with growing delay
questions.explanation: The container exits repeatedly; Kubernetes backs off exponentially. Check logs and the liveness probe.

questions.text: A PodDisruptionBudget protects workloads during…
options: Image vulnerability scans in practice as a rule
Secret rotation
Network partitions
Voluntary disruptions like node drains
questions.explanation: It caps how many pods may be down at once, so a node upgrade can't take the whole app offline.

questions.text: The Horizontal Pod Autoscaler scales based on…
options: The number of nodes in the cluster
The size of the container image
The time of day in practice as a rule in practice
Observed metrics compared against a target
questions.explanation: CPU, memory or custom metrics drive replica count toward the target you set.

questions.text: A container escaping to the host most often exploits…
options: A kernel vulnerability or an over-privileged container
A missing readiness probe
An oversized image layer
A weak container registry password
questions.explanation: Shared kernel plus --privileged or a mounted docker socket turns a container break-out into host control.

questions.text: In Kubernetes, taints and tolerations are used to…
options: Control which pods may schedule onto which nodes
Roll back a failed deployment in practice as a rule
Encrypt pod-to-pod traffic
Limit a pod's CPU usage
questions.explanation: A node taint repels pods unless they carry a matching toleration — how you reserve nodes for specific workloads.

questions.text: A service mesh (e.g. Istio, Linkerd) primarily adds…
options: Automatic image building
Persistent storage for stateful apps in practice as a rule
mTLS, traffic control and observability via sidecars
A faster container runtime
questions.explanation: Sidecar proxies handle encryption, routing and telemetry without changing application code.

questions.text: The CNI (Container Network Interface) is responsible for…
options: Configuring pod networking and IP allocation
Building container images reproducibly
Storing secrets securely at rest
Scheduling pods onto nodes
questions.explanation: Calico, Cilium and friends plug in here to give every pod an address and enforce network policy.

questions.text: A Kubernetes Operator extends the cluster by…
options: Replacing the default scheduler
Providing a web dashboard
Caching container images locally
Encoding operational knowledge as a custom controller
questions.explanation: A custom resource plus a controller automates day-2 tasks — backups, failover — the way a human operator would.

questions.text: etcd in a Kubernetes control plane stores…
options: Ingress TLS certificates only
Container image layers
The entire cluster state and configuration
Pod logs and metrics
questions.explanation: The single source of truth; losing etcd without a backup means losing the cluster's brain.

questions.text: A rolling update with maxUnavailable=0 and maxSurge=1 will…
options: Delete all old pods before creating new ones
Add a new pod before removing an old one, one at a time
Pause until manually approved in practice as a rule in practice
Update every pod simultaneously
questions.explanation: Zero downtime: capacity never dips below desired because a surge pod comes up before an old one goes.
